3: Livestock manure contains significant contents of nutrients and organic matter that are often used to increase crop yield and improve the chemical and physical properties of soil (Schlegel et al., 2017). However, the current trends in livestock production intensification encounter continuous manure discharge into the environment (Oenema et al., 2007). Organic matter is responsible for acute water pollution incidents and odour problems. Phospho- rus (P) and nitrogen (N) contaminate soil and surface waters, which may lead to eutrophication (Menzi et al., 2010). Conse- quently, European environmental regulations concerning the direct application of manure as fertilizer are increasing (Directive 91/676/EEC, 1991), limiting its use in regions where production is larger than the allowable demand for crop fertilization (Oenema et al., 2007). At the same time, the need of mineral N and P fertilizers and their production cost are steadily growing, jeopardizing the progressively exhausted deposits of phosphate rock (Vaccari, 2009). The combination of these two factors makes
the recovery of nutrients from animal manure crucial to protect the environment and to ensure a renewable source of fertilizers.

Anaerobic digestion (AD) or co-digestion (AcoD) is a widely implemented technology to remove organic matter from manure, which has the advantage of producing methane (CH4) as renewable energy (Nasir et al., 2012). Despite the fact that AD does not remove nutrients, it enhances their solubilisation (Tao et al., 2016); there- fore, digestate post-treatment is required to either remove or recover nutrients. Several technologies have been tested to fulfil this goal; some of them are still on an experimental level, for exam- ple, the recovery of ammonia (NH3) from swine manure through gas-permeable membranes (García-González and Vanotti, 2015), while others are developed enough to be implemented at industrial scale. Most digestate post-treatment processes start with physical solid/liquid separation. Centrifugation is the most applied option leading to an organic P-rich solid stream and a liquid N-rich cen- trate (Burton, 2007; Moller et al., 2007). However, although the solid fraction is often used as fertilizer, its composition is variable, and it is impossible to separate phosphorus from organic matter. Membrane filtration is used to concentrate nutrients in lower vol- ume streams. Additionally, it provides pathogen-free irrigation water and accomplishes limited N elimination but efficient P removal (Masse et al., 2007). When mainly P recovery is targeted, the precipitation of struvite (MgNH4PO4-6H20) can be considered as slow-release fertilizer (Karakashev et al., 2008). However, an

efficient solid/liquid separation step should always precede the struvite crystallizer to avoid interference of suspended solids with struvite precipitation. Conversely, if N recovery is pursued, ammo- nium sulphate production from ammonia stripping and subsequent scrubbing in sulphuric acid is the most applied technology (Bonmati and Flotats, 2003). But, when N recovery is not the final aim or when it is not complete, this nutrient is largely removed as N2 through the conventional initrification/denitrification process. Innovative processes, such as initritation/denitritation (Nit/Denit) and partial nitritation/anammox (PNA) can be performed if the biodegradable organic matter content is low, allowing energy sav- ings and minimizing sludge production (Daigger, 2014). The above-mentioned technologies can be combined (Karakashev et al., 2008) to address regional requirements (mainly regarding N-, vulnerabilities, or both), but the environmental implications related to each treatment configuration should also be assessed to guarantee a sustainable scheme. To do so, Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methodology is considered as a useful and widely applied tool to evaluate the environmental performance of a product or service by identifying critical stages in a supply chain where impacts could be reduced. In this sense, several LCA studies related to the AD of animal manure and the use of digestate for fertilization purposes have been published (Bacenetti et al., 2016). However, only a few go beyond those conventional strategies, focusing attention on the potential environmental advantages of nutrient recovery from digestate (Pardo et al., 2017; Rehl and Müller, 2011). In this context, the primary goal of this work was to study the environmental per- formance of seven technologically feasible treatment schemes aiming at energy and nutrient recovery from a mixture of livestock (pig and cow) manures.

7: Seven technologies (anaerobic digestion, acidification, centrifu- gation, membrane ultrafiltration, struvite precipitation, nitritation- denitritaton and High Rate Activated Sludge (HRAS) + PNA) were considered and tested in the operation of the ManureEcoMine pilot plant in Spain, with the exception of the biological N-removal pro- cesses. The precise description of the pilot plant can be found in Pintucci et al. (2017) and consisted of a 3 m³ anaerobic digester, an ammonia stripping column, a decanter centrifuge (MD-60, Lemitec Gmbh), an ultrafiltration system (UF F0701 Pilot skid, Likuid Nanotek) made of ceramic membranes with 100 nm pore size and a total filtrating surface of 1.08 m² and a 100 struvite reactor. The seven technologies chosen were combined into seven treatment schemes (scenarios, Fig. 1).

The input mixture was identical for all scenarios, and it was composed of 52% cow manure, 43% pig manure and 5% segregates (w/w). Both manures were provided by a farm located in Folguer- oles (Catalonia, Spain), while segregates is a sugar-rich product derived from the cleaning process in the food industry. The average physicochemical characteristics of the feeding mixture are shown in Table 1. A total of 100,000-ton feeding mixture per year, a rep- resentative value for medium-sized agro-industrial digesters (Bacenetti et al., 2016), were considered as the basis for compar- ison, resulting in a mass-based functional unit (FU) of 274 ton d-1.

Anaerobic stabilization was considered in all scenarios to reduce the environmental hazard of direct manure application, while at the same time allowing biogas production for energy needs (Nasir et al., 2012). The base scenario only included AD. In scenario 1, a centrifuge was added after AD to recover chemical oxygen demand (COD), total phosphorus (TP) and part of total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN) in the solid fraction, while centrate was

treated through nitrification via nitrite pathway (nitritation) fol- lowed by denitritation (Regmi et al., 2014) to remove N. The nov- elty of scenario 2 was the production of struvite from the centrate prior to N removal. Struvite crystallization was supposed to be fea- sible even in the presence of suspended solids in the centrate (Capdevielle et al., 2015; Tao et al., 2016), as in the pilot plant trials struvite was detected in the precipitate when the abovementioned influent was used. In scenario 3, an ultrafiltration unit was imple- mented after the centrifuge to improve struvite precipitation, and, at the same time, to facilitate the testing of biological N-removal not only through initritation/denitritation (option a), but also through partial initritation/anammox (option b) (Lackner et al., 2014). The high concentration of biodegradable organic matter in the permeate required a HRAS process before the anammox step to diminish the incoming Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) con- centration (Jenni et al., 2014). The only difference between scenar- ios 3 and 4 was the acidification step with the addition of sulphuric acid to the digestate before centrifugation aiming at increasing P solubility, thus augmenting its availability for struvite precipita- tion (Daumer et al., 2010).

In scenario 3, an ultrafiltration unit was imple- mented after the centrifuge to improve struvite precipitation, and, at the same time, to facilitate the testing of biological N-removal not only through initritation/denitritation (option a), but also through partial initritation/anammox (option b) (Lackner et al., 2014). The high concentration of biodegradable organic matter in the permeate required a HRAS process before the anammox step to diminish the incoming Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) con- centration (Jenni et al., 2014). The only difference between scenar- ios 3 and 4 was the acidification step with the addition of sulphuric acid to the digestate before centrifugation aiming at increasing P solubility, thus augmenting its availability for struvite precipita- tion (Daumer et al., 2010).
10: Since it is not possible to test at pilot scale all feasible scenarios, steady-state simulation is a powerful tool to generate detailed inventory data of a process integrating the experimental and liter- ature data of each single unit. This methodological approach was applied to perform solid, organic matter, N and P mass balances in the seven scenarios. The hypotheses taken to set the mass bal- ances are summarized in Table 2 and the average removal efficien- cies of total solids (TS), COD, TKN and TP in each unit are listed in Table S1. The performance of the decanter centrifuge depended on the pH of the digestate (Table S1), as observed in other studies (Daumer et al., 2010). Concerning BOD, no removal occurred beyond that of water partition (Burton, 2007). Also the removal efficiencies in the filtration unit depended on the pH value of the digestate (Narong and James, 2006). All soluble COD in the perme- ate was assumed as BOD. Pure sulphuric acid was dosed to the digestate before the centrifuge in scenario 4 to lower pH up to val- ues around 4.5-5, thus avoiding P precipitation in the solid/liquid separation step (Daumer et al., 2010). As a consequence, the buffer- ing capacity was mostly removed. The struvite crystallizer was designed according to Tarragó et al. (2016). As during the pilot plant operation struvite presence in the precipitate was detected but not quantified, the performance of this unit was assessed using the Visual Minteq 3.1 software, a chemical equilibrium model which, once the main ions present in an aqueous solution at a given pH and temperature are determined, can predict their chem- ical speciation and the solubility and/or precipitation of solid phases. Biological nitrogen removal was not conducted in the pilot plant, and thus, all data for mass balances come from literature (Table 2). Both initritation/denitritation unit and HRAS+PNA units worked as a sequencing batch reactor (SBR). In case of organic mat- ter deficiency in the nitritation/denitritation, BOD supplementa- tion (80% glycerol solution) was considered. If the buffering capacity was not sufficient, NaHCO3 dosage was assumed. In case that P was limiting, it was simulated to be supplied in form of phosphoric acid. Organic N was supposed not to be consumed dur- ing this biological process. In the HRAS+PNA combination, the first compartment was employed to lower the organic matter concen- tration in contact with anammox biomass (Daigger, 2014). In case that P was limiting, the dosage of phosphoric acid was again con- sidered. The nitrate produced during the anammox process was assumed to be consumed by the small portion of heterotrophic

13: The electricity requirements for the operation of pumping and mixing devices were estimated (Table 2) according to the con- sumption factors found in the literature (Metcalf et al., 2004). Con- sumption rates for blowing systems were also calculated taking into account the oxygen demand, the power of the equipment and the operation time (Metcalf et al., 2004). Electricity use for

centrifugation was estimated according to the ratio proposed by Pöschl et al. (2010). Also, heat consumption in anaerobic digester was calculated from the available information in the literature (Table 2), assuming 15 °C and 37 as initial and final temperature, respectively.

However, renewable energy from anaerobic digestion can par- tially offset the energy balance. Thus, biogas valorization was assumed to be performed using a cogeneration (CHP) unit to pro- duce both electricity (partially used for CHP operation) and heat (Pöschl et al., 2010). Biogas rate of 24 m³. 1 input was considered with a composition of 60% of CH4.
14: Both digestate and nutrient-rich fractions (solid fraction after centrifugation, retentate, struvite, excess sludge and final effluent) were assumed to be used as soil fertilizers replacing the production and subsequent use of synthetic ones to some extent (avoided mineral fertilizer production and on-site related emissions). In this regard, ammonium nitrate (NH4NO3) and phosphorus oxide (P2O5) were assumed to be partially substituted by conventional mineral fertilizers on the market. Default processes provided by the ecoin- vent® database v3.2 for these chemicals were considered for calcu- lations (Althaus et al., 2007). A mineral fertilizer replacement value ranging from 41% (solid products) to 62% (final effluent) was con- sidered for N from organic sources according to literature (De Vries et al., 2011, 2012); a replacement rate of 100% was applied in case of N from mineral struvite (Tao et al., 2016). Similarly, the uptake rate of P was assumed to vary between 95% (solid products, low- quality struvite and final effluent) and 99% (high-quality struvite),

mainly based on the degree of purity of the different products (Dalgaard et al., 2006; De Vries et al., 2011; Rahman et al., 2014). The avoided emissions from the substitution of mineral fertilizers were estimated in terms of dinitrogen monoxide (N20), nitrogen oxides (NOx) and NH3 concentrations, taking into account the methodology proposed by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC, 2006). Tier 2 method was followed as the basis for calculations, using both available primary data (mass flow, N and P content) from mass balances together with the default emission factors provided by the method (0.01 kg N-N20.kg N applied; 0.20 kg N-NH3+N-NOx.kg N applied: 99% N-NH3 vs 1% N-NOx). Nitrate (NO) leaching rate was accounted according to the factor of 0.30 kg N-NO.kg-1 N applied (IPCC, 2006) while phosphate (PO) emissions to water were evaluated based on the ratio of 0.01 kg P-PO- kg-1 of applied P proposed by Rossier (1998). How- ever, not only the environmental credits related to the use of such soil fertilizers were accounted for but also those direct impacts derived from their application on soils. Similarly, IPCC standards (Tier 2) were also followed to estimate emissions and discharges from the use of these recovered fertilizers.

2.4.2. Diffuse emissions from biogas losses and storage activities Around 1.5% of biogas losses from the anaerobic digester (1.0%) and gas engine (0.5%) were assumed as reported in De Vries et al. (2012). Similarly, emissions in terms of N20 and NO were also considered taking into account the average ratios of 0.62 mg N20 and 1.22 g NO per m³ of biogas generated (De Vries et al., 2012). Storage emissions were only considered from digestate (Base scenario) in uncovered conditions - as the most commonly wide- spread practice to date - during an average period of 150 days (Gioelli et al., 2011; Liebetrau et al., 2010; Rehl and Müller, 2011). The other fertilizer products (Scenarios 1 to 4b) were sup- posed to be stored in fully closed tanks during minimum storage times, so that negligible storage emissions were expected. An emission factor of 0.20 kg CH4.kg-1 COD was estimated in accor- dance with the Organic Waste Digestion Project Protocol (2009), which proposes a generic way to determine CH4 emissions regard- less of the storage period, while focusing on the COD content of the digestate and the potential CH4 yield. This factor would also be in line with the values reported in similar studies in literature (Gioelli et al., 2011) considering 150 days of storage time (as in the present work) and the volatile solids (VS) content of digestate from cattle slurry (and other farmyard manure). For N-based emissions and discharges (N20, NOx, NH3, NO), IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006) were considered again.

2.4.3. N20 emissions during the N removal stage N losses associated with N20 emissions were also accounted for the biological N removal units. Regarding initritation/denitritation process, an average value of 15.5% of influent N load typically found in the literature (Pijuan et al., 2014; Scaglione et al., 2013) was used. However, these N20 emissions are significantly lower (2%) when a PNA unit is adopted (Castro-Barros et al., 2015; Scaglione et al., 2015; Kampschreur et al., 2008).

Commercial chemicals were considered to be used for the cleaning of UF membranes. In this regard, diluted caustic soda (NaOH, 1% v/v) and nitric acid (HNO3, 0.5% v/v) were assumed to be applied together with an intermediate rinse with water, accord- ing to the suppliers' protocol. Default processes provided by the ecoinvent database v3.2 (Althaus et al., 2007) were used to inte- grate the environmental burdens derived from the production of
these cleaning products. However, it should be also noted that cleanings would be performed whenever the UF membrane unit was not operated for a period of more than 2 days.

mainly based on the degree of purity of the different products (Dalgaard et al., 2006; De Vries et al., 2011; Rahman et al., 2014). The avoided emissions from the substitution of mineral fertilizers were estimated in terms of dinitrogen monoxide (N20), nitrogen oxides (NOx) and NH3 concentrations, taking into account the methodology proposed by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC, 2006). Tier 2 method was followed as the basis for calculations, using both available primary data (mass flow, N and P content) from mass balances together with the default emission factors provided by the method (0.01 kg N-N20.kg N applied; 0.20 kg N-NH3+N-NOx.kg N applied: 99% N-NH3 vs 1% N-NOx). Nitrate (NO) leaching rate was accounted according to the factor of 0.30 kg N-NO.kg-1 N applied (IPCC, 2006) while phosphate (PO) emissions to water were evaluated based on the ratio of 0.01 kg P-PO- kg-1 of applied P proposed by Rossier (1998). How- ever, not only the environmental credits related to the use of such soil fertilizers were accounted for but also those direct impacts derived from their application on soils. Similarly, IPCC standards (Tier 2) were also followed to estimate emissions and discharges from the use of these recovered fertilizers.

2.4.2. Diffuse emissions from biogas losses and storage activities Around 1.5% of biogas losses from the anaerobic digester (1.0%) and gas engine (0.5%) were assumed as reported in De Vries et al. (2012). Similarly, emissions in terms of N20 and NO were also considered taking into account the average ratios of 0.62 mg N20 and 1.22 g NO per m³ of biogas generated (De Vries et al., 2012). Storage emissions were only considered from digestate (Base scenario) in uncovered conditions - as the most commonly wide- spread practice to date - during an average period of 150 days (Gioelli et al., 2011; Liebetrau et al., 2010; Rehl and Müller, 2011). The other fertilizer products (Scenarios 1 to 4b) were sup- posed to be stored in fully closed tanks during minimum storage times, so that negligible storage emissions were expected. An emission factor of 0.20 kg CH4.kg-1 COD was estimated in accor- dance with the Organic Waste Digestion Project Protocol (2009), which proposes a generic way to determine CH4 emissions regard- less of the storage period, while focusing on the COD content of the digestate and the potential CH4 yield. This factor would also be in line with the values reported in similar studies in literature (Gioelli et al., 2011) considering 150 days of storage time (as in the present work) and the volatile solids (VS) content of digestate from cattle slurry (and other farmyard manure). For N-based emissions and discharges (N20, NOx, NH3, NO), IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006) were considered again.

2.4.3. N20 emissions during the N removal stage N losses associated with N20 emissions were also accounted for the biological N removal units. Regarding initritation/denitritation process, an average value of 15.5% of influent N load typically found in the literature (Pijuan et al., 2014; Scaglione et al., 2013) was used. However, these N20 emissions are significantly lower (2%) when a PNA unit is adopted (Castro-Barros et al., 2015; Scaglione et al., 2015; Kampschreur et al., 2008).

Commercial chemicals were considered to be used for the cleaning of UF membranes. In this regard, diluted caustic soda (NaOH, 1% v/v) and nitric acid (HNO3, 0.5% v/v) were assumed to be applied together with an intermediate rinse with water, accord- ing to the suppliers' protocol. Default processes provided by the ecoinvent database v3.2 (Althaus et al., 2007) were used to inte- grate the environmental burdens derived from the production of
these cleaning products. However, it should be also noted that cleanings would be performed whenever the UF membrane unit was not operated for a period of more than 2 days.

The avoided emissions from the substitution of mineral fertilizers were estimated in terms of dinitrogen monoxide (N20), nitrogen oxides (NOx) and NH3 concentrations, taking into account the methodology proposed by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC, 2006). Tier 2 method was followed as the basis for calculations, using both available primary data (mass flow, N and P content) from mass balances together with the default emission factors provided by the method (0.01 kg N-N20.kg N applied; 0.20 kg N-NH3+N-NOx.kg N applied: 99% N-NH3 vs 1% N-NOx). Nitrate (NO) leaching rate was accounted according to the factor of 0.30 kg N-NO.kg-1 N applied (IPCC, 2006) while phosphate (PO) emissions to water were evaluated based on the ratio of 0.01 kg P-PO- kg-1 of applied P proposed by Rossier (1998). How- ever, not only the environmental credits related to the use of such soil fertilizers were accounted for but also those direct impacts derived from their application on soils. Similarly, IPCC standards (Tier 2) were also followed to estimate emissions and discharges from the use of these recovered fertilizers.

2.4.2. Diffuse emissions from biogas losses and storage activities Around 1.5% of biogas losses from the anaerobic digester (1.0%) and gas engine (0.5%) were assumed as reported in De Vries et al. (2012). Similarly, emissions in terms of N20 and NO were also considered taking into account the average ratios of 0.62 mg N20 and 1.22 g NO per m³ of biogas generated (De Vries et al., 2012). Storage emissions were only considered from digestate (Base scenario) in uncovered conditions - as the most commonly wide- spread practice to date - during an average period of 150 days (Gioelli et al., 2011; Liebetrau et al., 2010; Rehl and Müller, 2011). The other fertilizer products (Scenarios 1 to 4b) were sup- posed to be stored in fully closed tanks during minimum storage times, so that negligible storage emissions were expected. An emission factor of 0.20 kg CH4.kg-1 COD was estimated in accor- dance with the Organic Waste Digestion Project Protocol (2009), which proposes a generic way to determine CH4 emissions regard- less of the storage period, while focusing on the COD content of the digestate and the potential CH4 yield. This factor would also be in line with the values reported in similar studies in literature (Gioelli et al., 2011) considering 150 days of storage time (as in the present work) and the volatile solids (VS) content of digestate from cattle slurry (and other farmyard manure). For N-based emissions and discharges (N20, NOx, NH3, NO), IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006) were considered again.

2.4.3. N20 emissions during the N removal stage N losses associated with N20 emissions were also accounted for the biological N removal units. Regarding initritation/denitritation process, an average value of 15.5% of influent N load typically found in the literature (Pijuan et al., 2014; Scaglione et al., 2013) was used. However, these N20 emissions are significantly lower (2%) when a PNA unit is adopted (Castro-Barros et al., 2015; Scaglione et al., 2015; Kampschreur et al., 2008).

Commercial chemicals were considered to be used for the cleaning of UF membranes. In this regard, diluted caustic soda (NaOH, 1% v/v) and nitric acid (HNO3, 0.5% v/v) were assumed to be applied together with an intermediate rinse with water, accord- ing to the suppliers' protocol. Default processes provided by the ecoinvent database v3.2 (Althaus et al., 2007) were used to inte- grate the environmental burdens derived from the production of
these cleaning products. However, it should be also noted that cleanings would be performed whenever the UF membrane unit was not operated for a period of more than 2 days.
7: Cow and pig manures were assumed to be produced in nearby farms close to the plant facilities; in contrast, an average distance of 80 km was assumed for segregates, coming from a sugar factory (personal communication). Regarding the transportation of the dif- ferent fertilizer products (from plant facilities to agricultural soils), not only average distances but also the area needed for N applica- tion under safe conditions were estimated in compliance with European restrictions on N content in soils, in absence of a Euro- pean legislative framework concerning P application in agriculture (Amery and Schoumand, 2014). Consequently, transport activities were assumed to be directly affected by the properties of each fer- tilizer product, mainly in terms of their primary nutrients (N and P) and moisture content (digestate VS concentrated fertilizers). In this regard, 170 kg N.ha-¹ was considered to be the limit of N in nitrate vulnerable zones (NVZ) according to the Nitrates Directive 91/676/ EEC (1991), while P fertilization was indirectly limited by N regulation.
8: Secondary data from literature and commercial databases were used to complete the background inventory. In this sense, data about electricity requirements (Spanish electricity mix, medium voltage) and heat consumption (European profile from natural gas), diesel and other fossil fuels production, agrochemicals manu- facture and combustion emissions associated with fuel use by pilot plant machinery and transportation equipment were taken from ecoinvent database v3.2 (Althaus et al., 2007; Dones et al., 2007; Moreno Ruiz et al., 2015; Spielmann et al., 2007).

The following impact categories were selected in agreement with the characterization factors reported by the ReCiPe Midpoint (H) 1.12 method (Goedkoop et al., 2013): climate change (CC), terrestrial acidification (TA), freshwater eutrophication (FE), marine eutroph- ication (ME), water depletion (WD) and fossil depletion (FD). A brief description of these impact categories can be found in Table S16.A system expansion approach was followed to account for the val- orization of the different products (Fig. 1), which means that not only the environmental impacts associated with their production processes were taken into consideration but also the related envi- ronmental credits associated to their further valorization.
10: The global COD, N and P balances in the seven scenarios are shown in Fig. 2. More details about the inputs and outputs of each technology in each scenario are shown in Tables S2-S15. The inlet loads and the COD converted into energy were identical in all cases.

In the base scenario, only 48% of COD was recovered as energy due to CH4 production during anaerobic digestion. The remaining organic matter and all nutrients were discharged with the diges- tate. No addition of chemicals or oxygen was needed.

In scenario 1, 27% of the inlet COD, 33% of N and 83% of P were recovered in the solid fraction. During the initritation/denitritation step, around 40% of the inlet N was removed (corresponding to the total ammonium nitrogen fed to the biological reactor), while another 8% was consumed for biomass growth, thus present in the excess sludge. P was partially consumed in this last compart- ment (7% of inlet TP) and concentrated in the excess sludge. Organic nitrogen (27% of the inlet N) and the rest of TP (17%) remained in the final effluent. BOD was depleted as carbon source in the biological reactor, remaining only the non-biodegradable part of inlet COD (21%) in the final effluent. The presence of the bio- logical N removal step led to chemical (sodium bicarbonate and glycerol) and oxygen consumption (Fig. 2).

Scenario 2, compared to scenario 1, allowed to recover an addi- tional 2% of N, 9% of P and 2% of COD because of struvite precipita- tion. As a result, the final effluent of this treatment scheme presents lower levels of nutrients (only 25% of inlet N and 9% of inlet P remained in the outlet stream). The composition of excess sludge and the oxygen supply were similar to scenario 1, while the consumption of chemicals increased due to Mg(OH)2 dosing (Fig. 2).

The addition of an ultrafiltration step in scenario 3a allowed to recover 70% of inlet N, 49% of inlet COD and almost all TP in the solid fraction. Consequently, struvite production and N removal were lower than in scenario 2 VS. 162 kg.FU-1 and 26% VS. 40%, respectively). The introduction of a second solid/liquid separation step led to a final effluent with lower nutrient content (4% of the inlet N and negligible values of COD and P), and to a smaller production of excess sludge. Chemical and oxygen dosing were lower than in scenario 2 due to the smaller quantity of N and P entering the crystallizer and the biological reactor (Fig.

The choice of the combination HRAS+PNA in scenario 3b caused variations in excess sludge production, oxygen and chemical sup- ply and final effluent quality (mainly COD levels) compared to sce- nario 3a (Fig. 2). The faster growth of the heterotrophic microorganisms during the HRAS process explained the higher content in COD, N and P of the solid fraction (50% of inlet COD, 73% of inlet N and almost all P). The presence of this same process caused greater oxygen consumption and excess sludge production, while chemical dosing resulted lower regarding NaHCO3 and glyc- erol. Since the first biological compartment in scenario 3b aimed to decrease the BOD/N ratio and not to eliminate organic matter com- pletely, the COD content in the final effluent (1.5% of the inlet COD) was higher than in scenario 3a.

The addition of sulphuric acid in scenarios 4a and 4b influenced not only chemical consumption (from 8- to 10-fold higher than in scenarios 3), but also the efficiencies of the downstream technolo- gies. The performance of the solid/liquid separation steps changed, confirming the results obtained by other studies (Narong and James, 2006; Daumer et al., 2010; Fangueiro et al., 2015), since N and P remained mostly in the liquid fraction: only 47% of inlet N and 57% of inlet P were recovered in the solid fraction. The higher concentrations of nutrients entering the struvite reactor led to an increased struvite production (749 kg FU-1. However, sodium bicarbonate addition in scenarios 4 was almost 6 times higher than in scenarios 3 to restore the eliminated alkalinity during the acid- ification step. In the biological reactor, excess sludge production and oxygen supply (Fig. 2) in scenario 4a were higher than in sce- nario 3a, because of the higher content of ammonium to be removed; on the contrary, they were lower in scenario 4b than in scenario 3b due to the smaller quantity of BOD entering the sys- tem. In both scenarios, nutrient presence in the final effluent was higher (11% of the inlet N).
A global comparison among the six scenarios pursuing N removal and/or P recovery is shown in Figs. S1-S5. It can be observed that scenarios 1 and 2 have similar inputs and outputs

In the biological reactor, excess sludge production and oxygen supply (Fig. 2) in scenario 4a were higher than in sce- nario 3a, because of the higher content of ammonium to be removed; on the contrary, they were lower in scenario 4b than in scenario 3b due to the smaller quantity of BOD entering the sys- tem. In both scenarios, nutrient presence in the final effluent was higher (11% of the inlet N).

26: <header>3.2.1. Base scenario (SO) - Energy recovery from manure </header>

Relative contributions of involved activities showed diffuse emissions from both storage and fertilization activities (C and N emissions) as one of the main factors responsible for the environ- mental impact of base scenario (Fig. 3). More specifically, C (mainly CH4) emissions from digestate storage accounted for 35% of the CC impacts and N (direct and indirect) emissions from digestate appli- cation to soils are the major contributors in TA and FE. The low level of chemicals and energy requirements in this scheme led to minor contribution from these factors. Conversely, the generation of renewable energy from biogas combustion (avoided energy pro- duction) and the recovery of nutrients in the digestate stream (avoided fertilizers production) had a favourable influence on the environmental profile (especially in terms of CC, FE, WD and FD). In this way, the environmental balance of base scenario was par- tially offset in most impact categories, except for TA and ME due to the most considerable damaging effect of N emissions for the environment.
27: Fig. 3 demonstrated how environmental impacts from diffuse (C and N) emissions are decisive for most impact categories (CC, TA, ME). N losses in terms of N20 emissions from both nitrification/ denitrification stage and final fertilization activities exerted the main influence. Conversely, C emissions were minimal since negli- gible storage emissions were assumed to be generated in scenario 1. Chemicals use and energy consumption also had an important role in the environmental impacts (FE, WD and FD). The use of NaHCO3 in the biological N removal process was responsible for most environmental burdens in FE and FD, whereas electricity requirements from both centrifugation stage and oxygen supply units (blowers) had a significant influence on ME and WD. The recovered products in terms of solid fraction (from the decanter centrifuge) and the excess sludge (from the nitrification/denitrifica tion) unit were assumed to be applied to agricultural soils as nutri- ents source; similarly, final effluent was also considered to be used as irrigation water in agriculture. Thus, related avoided impacts resulted in significant environmental credits which, together with the generation and use of renewable energy, made it possible to significantly counteract the environmental impacts of scenario 1 in some impact categories.
28: <header>3.2.3. Scenario 2 - Energy recovery + Poor-quality struvite + N removal </header>

The implementation of struvite precipitation did not influence the environmental results decisively compared to Scenario 1 (below 1% in most impact categories), which can be explained by the relatively low struvite production rate (0.60 kg.t input). Only a relevant effect could be observed in WD (higher chemicals

production influence) due to the dosage of Mg(OH)2 solution to meet the stoichiometric requirements for struvite precipitation. Both N emissions and electricity consumption continue to have a critical impact, while renewable energy generation from biogas combustion and valorisation of recovered products were responsi- ble for the greatest environmental credits (Fig. 3).

3.2.4. Scenarios 3a/3b - Membrane filtration + High-quality struvite The implementation of the ultrafiltration membranes in scenar- ios 3 seeks to upgrade the struvite quality as well as to enable N removal through HRAS+PNA (scenario 3b). However, the amount of struvite was so minimal (~0.02 kg t ¹ input) that the environ- mental credits associated with its improvement in quality (99% P substitution capacity) were practically negligible (Fig. 3). Conse- quently, major differences of scenarios 3 compared to scenario 2 were related to final N removal. In this stage, lower rates of chem- icals were required, so that environmental impacts associated with the use of these chemicals were reduced (Fig. 3). Differences between scenarios 3a and 3b were mainly associated to N emis- sions from the final N removal stage, directly related to CC con- cerns. The implementation of PNA allows reducing up to 80% these emissions so that the overall balance resulted in more favourable environmental results for scenario 3b compared to sce- nario 3a (Fig. 3).

The implementation of struvite precipitation did not influence the environmental results decisively compared to Scenario 1 (below 1% in most impact categories), which can be explained by the relatively low struvite production rate (0.60 kg.t input). production influence) due to the dosage of Mg(OH)2 solution to meet the stoichiometric requirements for struvite precipitation. Both N emissions and electricity consumption continue to have a critical impact, while renewable energy generation from biogas combustion and valorisation of recovered products were responsi- ble for the greatest environmental credits (Fig. 3).

3.2.4. Scenarios 3a/3b - Membrane filtration + High-quality struvite The implementation of the ultrafiltration membranes in scenar- ios 3 seeks to upgrade the struvite quality as well as to enable N removal through HRAS+PNA (scenario 3b). However, the amount of struvite was so minimal (~0.02 kg t ¹ input) that the environ- mental credits associated with its improvement in quality (99% P substitution capacity) were practically negligible (Fig. 3). Conse- quently, major differences of scenarios 3 compared to scenario 2 were related to final N removal. In this stage, lower rates of chem- icals were required, so that environmental impacts associated with the use of these chemicals were reduced (Fig. 3). Differences between scenarios 3a and 3b were mainly associated to N emis- sions from the final N removal stage, directly related to CC con- cerns. The implementation of PNA allows reducing up to 80% these emissions so that the overall balance resulted in more favourable environmental results for scenario 3b compared to sce- nario 3a (Fig. 3).
30: <header>3.2.5. Scenarios 4a/4b - Contribution of the acidification stage </header>

From an environmental perspective, the implementation of the acidification stage prior to centrifugation increased the amount of struvite produced (more than 100 times higher than in scenarios 3), and consequently, the environmental credits derived from its use as fertilizer. However, these environmental credits minimally offset the impacts derived from the substantially higher chemical requirements (H2SO4, Mg(OH)2, NaHCO3...) Consequently, the net balance led to very unfavourable environmental results, especially in those impact categories in which the use of industrial chemicals exerts a greater influence (eutrophication and resources depletion). Similar to scenarios 3a and 3b, the differences between scenarios 4a and 4b lie in the final N removal step, which mainly affects the results of CC, due to the lower N20 emissions in scenario 4b (Fig. 3).
31: LCA results of the different scenarios were compared for all impact categories (Fig. 4). It can be observed that the net environ- mental balance of the base scenario (SO) led to the most favourable results in CC (due to low N emissions), FE and FD (both supported by the lower energy and chemical requirements). However, the base scenario should also not be taken as the most desirable alter- native based on current reality and prospects in most European regions. According to the Directive 91/676/EEC (1991), the best management practices for N and P use on agricultural soils must be prioritised to avoid major environmental consequences, e.g., freshwater eutrophication (Bomans et al., 2005). Therefore, although the direct application would be the simplest alternative for digestate valorisation, this scenario would be possible when digestate can be applied on nearby agricultural soils to avoid impacts from transportation activities and with no restrictions in terms of N, P and organic matter, since all three are combined in a single product and not always in the right proportion. However, alternative options to manage digestate nutrients were demonstrated to be available when the direct application is not possible, although the preference for one scenario or another will be highly dependent on the particular boundary conditions of each site. 34: The base scenario, where only energy is recovered and digestate is applied to soils, has not such an unfavourable environmental performance as it might be expected. However, legal restrictions on N (and P) ratios dispersed on soils limits its implementation. Strategies pursuing P recovery as high-quality struvite through digestate acidification prior to a two-step solid/liquid separation are environmentally interesting alternatives if HRAS/PNA is used for N removal. On the contrary, when P recovery is not mandatory, a simpler treatment scheme composed of energy recovery, cen- trifugation and N removal by nitritation/denitritation is recom- mended. Further research should stress economic and social perspectives.
